news_summary: Today's human rights news highlights the empowerment of women through research, safe public spaces, and organized efforts that strengthen democracy and advance gender justice globally.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité des droits humains met aujourd'hui en avant l'autonomisation des femmes grâce à la recherche, à la sécurité des espaces publics et aux efforts concertés qui renforcent la démocratie et font progresser la justice entre les sexes à l'échelle mondiale.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan el empoderamiento de las mujeres a través de la investigación, los espacios públicos seguros y los esfuerzos organizados que fortalecen la democracia y promueven la justicia de género a nivel mundial.

articles.title: Celebrate women in research and the networks that sustain them
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00761-zOn International Women’s Day, we celebrate technology pioneers and recognize the mentorship that is necessary to inspire the current generation and those of the future.
articles.content:
On International Women’s Day, we celebrate technology pioneers and recognize the mentorship that is necessary to inspire the current generation and those of the future.

Zhen Xu, Ellen Roche and Xiwen Gong (left to right) are this year’s recipients of the Sony Women in Technology Award with Nature.Credit: Sony

On International Women’s Day (8 March), researchers around the world will honour women’s contributions to research. At Nature, we have launched a special collection of articles recognizing remarkable individuals. We also report on some of the challenges that stand in the way of true gender equality, and feature research developments that could make a difference, especially to women’s health.

International Women’s Day

All women in research deserve recognition for the valuable and varied contributions they make — and not just on International Women’s Day. In this Editorial, we highlight three outstanding researchers. They are the recipients of the 2026 Sony Women in Technology Award with Nature, announced last month at a ceremony in Tokyo. The three prizes recognize outstanding research that benefits society and the planet.

Xiwen Gong, a chemical engineer at the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor, was awarded the early-career prize for her research in materials with uses from renewable energy to health care. One of Gong’s interests is solar cells made from a type of crystalline material called a perovskite. These solar cells work similarly to silicon-based ones: they produce electrons after absorbing light. A key difference, however, is that the main light-absorbing layer is the hybrid organic–inorganic perovskites, rather than silicon.

These perovskites have potential advantages over silicon, because they produce electrons more efficiently and the layers of light-absorbing material can be thinner. This means that they should require less energy to manufacture and, ultimately, will be cheaper to produce. But they are less stable: their structure can change after exposure to factors such as light, heat and moisture, making them less reliable than silicon solar cells.

Women in science are not a ‘problem to be fixed’

Gong and her colleagues have been working to improve perovskite-cell stability in real-world conditions, for instance, by incorporating additives of various shapes and sizes and measuring their effects. In 2024, they found that bulky additives lead to fewer structural defects than less bulky ones do1.

In the mid-career category, the judges recognized biomedical engineer Ellen Roche at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge for her studies in advancing implantable and wearable medical devices. One such device is designed to assist the heart muscle in people with heart failure by mimicking the organ’s physiological contractions2. Another is a proof of concept for a device intended to replace or complement mechanical ventilation in people who have lost the function of their diaphragm as a result of progressive neuromuscular diseases3.

Our second mid-career winner is Zhen Xu, a biomedical engineer and surgeon at the University of Michigan. She has been recognized for co-inventing a technology called histotripsy. It involves using ultrasound to perform non-invasive surgery targeting tumour tissues in organs such as the liver. The technology works by generating controlled clouds of oscillating microbubbles through a process known as cavitation. These microbubbles pulverize cells in a targeted way, without producing heat or ionizing radiation. In 2023, more than ten years after Xu’s discovery4, histotripsy was approved by the US Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of liver tumours.

On the shoulders of giants

Although they are working on diverse problems, these three scientists share a commitment to collaboration and working across disciplines. The fact that today’s pioneers stand on the shoulders of giants is also highlighted in a Nature Careers Feature. In it, we asked female researchers who had previously won the Sony award, or other Nature prizes, to nominate colleagues who bring out the best in them.

‘No one quite like her’: meet the female colleagues who inspire these award-winning women in science

Epidemiologist Chelsea Polis at the Guttmacher Institute in New York City, winner of the 2023 John Maddox Prize, which recognizes science in the face of adversity, writes of her mentor Onikepe Owolabi, the institute’s vice-president of research: “What truly sets Oni apart is how she lifts others up. She is a deep listener who champions other people’s ideas, encourages colleagues to believe in themselves and inspires them with her own bold ideas and encyclopedic knowledge. Oni has changed the course of my life and career in multiple ways.”

Yating Wan — an electrical and computer engineer at the King Abdullah University of Science and Technology in Thuwal, Saudi Arabia, and one of the winners of last year’s Sony awards — writes of her doctoral supervisor Kei May Lau at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology: “She truly cares about her students’ growth. She often says that her proudest achievement is her students and postdocs, and she has always pushed us to aim higher than we thought we could.”

All of our winners, and those who have mentored and supported them, have advanced cutting-edge science and are working to improve the lives of others. On this International Women’s Day, we celebrate them and recognize that good science comes from generous mentorship, collaboration and mutual support. Without that recognition, we risk failing not only these inspirational researchers, but also science itself.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the recognition and celebration of women researchers who have made significant contributions to technology and health, emphasizing mentorship and gender equality in science. It reports on tangible achievements with broad societal impact, such as advancements in renewable energy and medical devices, and promotes the importance of supporting women in research, which is inspiring and meaningful for society at large.
articles.personality_title: Women researchers honored for breakthroughs in technology and health
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – On International Women’s Day, people worldwide recognize the important work women do in research. This day highlights both their achievements and the support they receive through mentorship and teamwork.

**What happened** – Three women received the 2026 Sony Women in Technology Award for their outstanding research. Xiwen Gong from the University of Michigan works on improving solar cells made from perovskites, which could make solar energy cheaper and more efficient. Ellen Roche from MIT develops medical devices to help people with heart problems and breathing difficulties. Zhen Xu, also from the University of Michigan, co-invented a new ultrasound surgery method called histotripsy that treats liver tumors without invasive surgery.

**Impact** – These awards show how women are leading important advances in science and technology that can improve energy use and health care. Their work also highlights the need for teamwork and mentorship, as many successful researchers credit others who guided and supported them. Recognizing these women helps encourage more girls and young women to pursue science.

**What’s next step** – The research by these women will continue to develop, potentially leading to cheaper solar panels, better medical devices, and safer, less invasive cancer treatments. The celebration also aims to strengthen networks of support for women in science, helping future researchers succeed.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Celebrating women researchers and their mentors shines a light on important scientific breakthroughs and the teamwork behind them, inspiring future generations to contribute to science and technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des femmes chercheuses récompensées pour des avancées en technologie et santé
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – À l’occasion de la Journée internationale des femmes, le monde entier célèbre le travail important des femmes dans la recherche. Cette journée met en avant leurs réalisations ainsi que le soutien qu’elles reçoivent grâce au mentorat et au travail d’équipe.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Trois femmes ont reçu le prix Sony Women in Technology 2026 pour leurs recherches remarquables. Xiwen Gong, de l’Université du Michigan, travaille sur l’amélioration des cellules solaires à base de pérovskites, qui pourraient rendre l’énergie solaire moins chère et plus efficace. Ellen Roche, du MIT, développe des dispositifs médicaux pour aider les personnes souffrant de problèmes cardiaques et respiratoires. Zhen Xu, également de l’Université du Michigan, a co-inventé une méthode chirurgicale par ultrasons appelée histotripsie, qui traite les tumeurs du foie sans chirurgie invasive.

**Impact** – Ces récompenses montrent comment les femmes dirigent d’importants progrès en science et technologie pouvant améliorer l’énergie et la santé. Leur travail souligne aussi l’importance du travail d’équipe et du mentorat, car beaucoup de chercheuses reconnaissent le soutien reçu. Mettre en lumière ces femmes encourage davantage de filles et de jeunes femmes à se lancer dans la science.

**Prochaine étape** – Les recherches de ces femmes continueront à évoluer, avec des avancées possibles vers des panneaux solaires moins chers, de meilleurs dispositifs médicaux et des traitements contre le cancer plus sûrs et moins invasifs. Cette célébration vise aussi à renforcer les réseaux de soutien aux femmes en science pour aider les futures chercheuses.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Célébrer les femmes chercheuses et leurs mentors met en lumière des découvertes scientifiques importantes et le travail d’équipe qui les soutient, inspirant les générations futures à contribuer à la science et à la technologie.
articles.personality_title_es: Mujeres investigadoras premiadas por avances en tecnología y salud
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el Día Internacional de la Mujer, en todo el mundo se reconoce el importante trabajo que hacen las mujeres en la investigación. Este día destaca tanto sus logros como el apoyo que reciben a través del mentorazgo y el trabajo en equipo.

**Qué pasó** – Tres mujeres recibieron el Premio Sony Women in Technology 2026 por sus investigaciones destacadas. Xiwen Gong, de la Universidad de Michigan, trabaja en mejorar las células solares hechas con perovskitas, que podrían hacer la energía solar más barata y eficiente. Ellen Roche, del MIT, desarrolla dispositivos médicos para ayudar a personas con problemas cardíacos y respiratorios. Zhen Xu, también de la Universidad de Michigan, co-inventó un método quirúrgico por ultrasonido llamado histotripsis, que trata tumores de hígado sin cirugía invasiva.

**Impacto** – Estos premios muestran cómo las mujeres lideran avances importantes en ciencia y tecnología que pueden mejorar el uso de energía y la atención médica. Su trabajo también destaca la importancia del trabajo en equipo y el mentorazgo, ya que muchas investigadoras reconocen el apoyo recibido. Reconocer a estas mujeres anima a más niñas y jóvenes a seguir carreras científicas.

**Próximo paso** – La investigación de estas mujeres seguirá desarrollándose, con posibilidad de lograr paneles solares más baratos, mejores dispositivos médicos y tratamientos contra el cáncer más seguros y menos invasivos. La celebración también busca fortalecer las redes de apoyo para mujeres en la ciencia, ayudando a futuras investigadoras.

**Frase clave** – Celebrar a las investigadoras y sus mentoras destaca importantes avances científicos y el trabajo en equipo detrás de ellos, inspirando a futuras generaciones a contribuir a la ciencia y la tecnología.
articles.image_prompt:
Three pioneering women researchers in technology and biomedical engineering; mentorship and collaboration as a nurturing network; innovative scientific advancements symbolized by perovskite solar cells, implantable medical devices, and ultrasound microbubble technology. These elements can be represented metaphorically by three interconnected glowing orbs or spheres, each containing abstract, stylized representations of their fields: a crystalline lattice pattern for perovskite solar cells, a delicate mechanical heart-like structure for medical devices, and dynamic swirling microbubbles for ultrasound therapy, all linked by gentle, flowing lines symbolizing mentorship and support. The composition radiates warmth and unity, using soft natural tones and a painterly style to evoke inspiration and collective progress in women’s scientific achievements.

Image Prompt: A warm, detailed painting of three interconnected glowing spheres—one with a crystalline lattice pattern, one with a delicate mechanical heart shape, and one filled with swirling microbubbles—linked by flowing, ribbon-like lines symbolizing mentorship, rendered in soft natural colors with an abstract, symbolic style.

articles.title: Radio Begum: A rare public space for women in Afghanistan
articles.summary: Every morning in Kabul, several cars make their way across the Afghan capital to pick up the producers of Radio Begum. The young women do not travel to the office on their own as moving around the city has become too complicated.
articles.content:
“They don’t come on their own by bus or taxi because it’s very complicated for a woman to move around the city, especially for young women,” station founder Hamida Anan told UN News, explaining the laws preventing them from doing so.

Once they arrive at the studio, the journalists hold their editorial meeting, prepare their shows and go live on air.

‘Glimmer of hope in the darkness’

The station, which receives support from the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), operates with a team of around 30 women and broadcasts across much of the country, except in about a dozen of Afghanistan’s 34 provinces, where authorities have banned even the sound of women’s voices in the media.

“Right now, when you are in Afghanistan and you change the channel on television or switch between radio stations, you only hear men’s voices or see images of men,” Ms. Anan says.

In this soundscape dominated by male voices, Radio Begum stands out.

“Hearing a woman’s voice in this entirely male universe is like a small light, a glimmer in an ocean of darkness.”

A radio station for women, by women

Radio Begum was launched in March 2021, just a few months before the Taliban returned to power.

Its founder, Ms. Anan, was born in Kabul but fled the war with her family at the age of eight and grew up in Switzerland, where she studied journalism. After the fall of the Taliban regime in 2001, she returned to her country to support the development of Afghan media.

In its early days, the station broadcast music, entertainment programmes and interviews with active women, highlighting the achievements of Afghan women over the past twenty years.

“Radio Begum is a radio station made for women, by women.”

But, after the Taliban takeover in August 2021, media outlets had to quickly adapt their content.

“Overnight, we had to stop broadcasting music. Overnight, we had to scale back our entertainment programmes.”

© Sandra Caligaro Once they arrive at the studio, Radio Begum journalists hold their editorial meeting, prepare their shows and go live on air.

Navigating new restrictions

Over the months, restrictions imposed on women and the media have multiplied. Women have gradually been excluded from many public-sector jobs, and female journalists must work under strict conditions: they can interview only women and cannot be alone in a studio with a man.

“There were constant warnings and threats,” Ms. Anan recalled.

To continue broadcasting, the station chose to avoid any political confrontation.

“We decided not to do politics. That is one of the reasons why we can continue to work.”

At the end of 2024, a decree issued by the Taliban authorities also declared it “improper” for women’s voices to be heard in public spaces, a decision that led several provinces to ban female voices from radio and television broadcasts.

“We are a radio station serving women,” Ms. Anan said. “We are no longer an ordinary media outlet.”

In this context, Radio Begum gradually adapted its programming and turned early toward education.

“We were pioneers using our airwaves for education.”

As early as autumn 2021, the station began broadcasting lessons well before the ban on girls attending school became widespread. When schools later closed to teenage girls, this mission became central.

“They closed the schools, yes. School is forbidden, but education is not. So, we will bring school into the home as much as possible.”

Today, six hours of educational programming based on the Afghan school curriculum are broadcast every day, three hours in Dari and three in Pashto..

The station also airs programmes on topics including health, psychological support, medical advice, spirituality, women’s entrepreneurship and social issues such as addiction. Most are broadcast live, allowing listeners to call in and ask questions.

Promoting women’s rights through Islam

To talk about women’s rights, Radio Begum has taken an unexpected path: religious texts.

“We inform women about their rights, and we use Islam to do that because it’s the only way,” Ms. Anan explained, adding that the station’s religious programme draws on verses, surahs and hadiths from the Qur’an, explained on air by female theologians.

“Islam is very precise about the place of women in society,” she said, citing rules concerning inheritance, divorce, the situation of widows and education. “We quote the verses, the surahs… so they can’t say anything.”

Initially scrutinised by authorities who wanted to ensure the hosts truly understood the religious texts, but their reaction surprised the editorial team.

“They told us it was their favourite programme.”

Today, the show ranks among the station’s most listened-to programmes.

© Sandra Caligaro Radio Begum broadcasts programmes on health, psychological support, spirituality, women’s entrepreneurship and social issues, many aired live with listeners able to call in and ask questions.

‘My husband behaves much better’

Each programme receives many calls from listeners across the country.

“The calls from listeners are a very good barometer of the impact of our programmes.”

Because of demand, some shows, particularly those focused on psychological support, have even been extended.

One listener from Bamiyan province said she learned about her inheritance rights through a programme and was able to assert them within her family.

In another case, a woman explained that listening to a show had changed her husband’s behaviour.

“My husband listened to the programme and since then, he behaves much better and he is much kinder.”

These testimonies, Ms. Anan says, “encourage us and give us a bit of comfort”.

‘We have to step in’

Despite these small advances, reality remains difficult.

“Being an Afghan woman means a lot of constraints and a lot of worries,” Ms. Anan says.

In this context, Radio Begum seeks to offer a rare space for expression and listening.

“We are responding to needs that the government should provide for women, but since this government has decided to ignore 50 per cent of its population, we have to step in.”

In a country where women are increasingly banned from the public sphere, Radio Begum continues to broadcast, providing a rare space where women can still be heard.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes Radio Begum, a women-led radio station in Afghanistan that provides education, psychological support, and promotes women's rights through religious texts despite severe restrictions under Taliban rule. It offers a rare and impactful public space for women in a highly oppressive environment, benefiting a broad segment of Afghan society by fostering education and empowerment.
articles.personality_title: Radio Begum: A rare voice for Afghan women under Taliban rule
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Afghanistan, women face many new restrictions under Taliban rule, including limits on their movement and bans on women’s voices in public media in some provinces. Many women find it difficult to work or be heard in the public space.

**What happened** – Radio Begum, a radio station run by women in Kabul, continues to broadcast programs for women despite these challenges. Founded in 2021 by Hamida Anan, the station now focuses on education, health, psychological support, and women’s rights, using Islamic teachings to explain women’s roles and rights. Around 30 female journalists work under strict rules, and the station broadcasts six hours of daily lessons based on Afghanistan’s school curriculum.

**Impact** – Radio Begum offers one of the few places where Afghan women’s voices can still be heard. It helps women learn about their rights, education, and health while providing a safe space for listeners to call in and ask questions. Some listeners have shared how the programs changed their lives, like a woman who learned about her inheritance rights or another whose husband became kinder after listening.

**What’s next step** – The station plans to keep adapting to the difficult environment and continue its educational and support programs. Its team hopes to reach more women across Afghanistan and maintain this rare public space where women’s voices are respected and heard.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Radio Begum stands out as a rare and vital radio station where Afghan women can learn, speak, and be heard despite growing restrictions under Taliban rule.
articles.personality_title_fr: Radio Begum : une voix rare pour les femmes afghanes sous le régime taliban
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Afghanistan, les femmes font face à de nombreuses nouvelles restrictions sous le régime taliban, notamment des limites sur leurs déplacements et des interdictions de diffuser la voix des femmes dans certains médias publics. Beaucoup de femmes ont du mal à travailler ou à se faire entendre en public.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Radio Begum, une radio dirigée par des femmes à Kaboul, continue d’émettre des programmes pour les femmes malgré ces défis. Fondée en 2021 par Hamida Anan, la station se concentre désormais sur l’éducation, la santé, le soutien psychologique et les droits des femmes, en utilisant les enseignements islamiques pour expliquer leurs rôles et droits. Environ 30 journalistes féminines travaillent sous des règles strictes, et la station diffuse six heures de cours quotidiens basés sur le programme scolaire afghan.

**Impact** – Radio Begum offre l’un des rares endroits où la voix des femmes afghanes peut encore être entendue. Elle aide les femmes à apprendre leurs droits, à s’instruire et à prendre soin de leur santé, tout en offrant un espace sûr où les auditrices peuvent appeler et poser des questions. Certaines ont raconté comment les émissions ont changé leur vie, comme une femme qui a appris ses droits à l’héritage ou une autre dont le mari est devenu plus gentil après avoir écouté.

**Prochaine étape** – La station prévoit de continuer à s’adapter à ce contexte difficile et à poursuivre ses programmes éducatifs et de soutien. Son équipe espère toucher davantage de femmes à travers l’Afghanistan et maintenir cet espace rare où la voix des femmes est respectée et entendue.

**En une phrase** – Radio Begum se distingue comme une radio rare et essentielle où les femmes afghanes peuvent apprendre, s’exprimer et se faire entendre malgré les restrictions croissantes du régime taliban.
articles.personality_title_es: Radio Begum: una voz rara para las mujeres afganas bajo el régimen talibán
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Afganistán, las mujeres enfrentan muchas nuevas restricciones bajo el régimen talibán, incluyendo límites para moverse y prohibiciones para que se escuchen sus voces en algunos medios públicos. Muchas mujeres tienen dificultades para trabajar o ser escuchadas en espacios públicos.

**Qué pasó** – Radio Begum, una estación de radio dirigida por mujeres en Kabul, sigue transmitiendo programas para mujeres a pesar de estos desafíos. Fundada en 2021 por Hamida Anan, la emisora ahora se enfoca en educación, salud, apoyo psicológico y derechos de las mujeres, usando enseñanzas islámicas para explicar sus roles y derechos. Unas 30 periodistas trabajan bajo reglas estrictas, y la estación transmite seis horas diarias de lecciones basadas en el currículo escolar afgano.

**Impacto** – Radio Begum ofrece uno de los pocos espacios donde las voces de las mujeres afganas todavía pueden escucharse. Ayuda a las mujeres a aprender sobre sus derechos, educación y salud, y ofrece un lugar seguro para que las oyentes llamen y hagan preguntas. Algunas han contado cómo los programas cambiaron sus vidas, como una mujer que aprendió sobre sus derechos de herencia o otra cuyo esposo se volvió más amable después de escuchar.

**Próximo paso** – La estación planea seguir adaptándose al difícil entorno y continuar con sus programas educativos y de apoyo. Su equipo espera alcanzar a más mujeres en todo Afganistán y mantener este espacio raro donde se respetan y escuchan las voces femeninas.

**Una frase clave** – Radio Begum destaca como una estación vital y rara donde las mujeres afganas pueden aprender, hablar y ser escuchadas a pesar de las crecientes restricciones del régimen talibán.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a traditional Afghan radio microphone glowing softly in a dim studio, surrounded by gentle silhouettes of women’s voices depicted as colorful sound waves weaving through a dark cityscape, symbolizing a rare beacon of female expression and education amidst restrictive shadows.

articles.title: When women organise, democracy survives: Why gender justice cannot be a casualty of global crises
articles.summary: “Across regions, feminist movements and women-led civil society organisations face a convergence of crises that threatens decades of hard-won progress ... ”
articles.content:
By Christelle Kalhoule and Sarah Strack

This March, as the world marks International Women’s Day, women’s rights organizations are not waiting to be recognised — they are leading movements, defending rights, and redefining what democracy looks like. Yet, even as they drive change and hold societies together, they are confronting an increasingly hostile environment. Across regions, feminist movements and women-led civil society organisations face a convergence of crises that threatens decades of hard-won progress: a shrinking enabling environment, rising authoritarianism, digital repression, and a global funding landscape retreating precisely when it is most needed.

This is a democratic issue, not a marginal one

From Sudan to Afghanistan, from Guatemala to Senegal, women’s organisations are often the first to respond when institutions fail — providing protection, documenting abuses, mediating conflicts, defending land and labour rights and holding power to account. Yet today, many of these organisations are being systematically pushed to the margins, treated as optional rather than essential actors in social and political life.

A perfect storm for feminist civil society

Over the last two years, women-led and gender-focused organisations have been disproportionately affected by funding cuts and shifting donor priorities. As resources are redirected toward short-term humanitarian responses, security, or geopolitical interests, long-term investments in gender justice, women’s leadership and feminist organising are being deprioritised or framed as “non-essential.”

At the same time, the enabling environment for civil society is shrinking at an alarming rate. Laws restricting rights of association, assembly and expression are increasingly used against feminist and LGBTQ+ movements. Women human rights defenders face surveillance, online harassment, criminalisation and physical threats — the lack of protection or accountability is often alarming.

In digital spaces, the backlash is just as real. Algorithms amplify misogyny, online violence silences women’s voices, and new technologies are deployed without safeguards for gender equality or human rights. Yet feminist organisations are rarely at the table where digital governance decisions are made.

This combination — less funding, fewer freedoms and greater risk — is forcing many organisations into survival mode. And when feminist civil society is weakened, societies become more unequal, more polarised and less democratic.

Gender justice is not a side issue

Despite this reality, gender equality is still too often treated as a thematic add-on or a box-ticking exercise, rather than a core political priority.

But the evidence is clear: when women organise, communities are more resilient. When feminist movements are included, policies are more inclusive. On the contrary, when women’s voices are silenced, democratic backsliding accelerates.

Gender justice is inseparable from civic freedom. There can be no meaningful democracy without the right to organise, protest, speak and dissent — and there can be no genuine enabling environment for civil society if women, girls or marginalised gender groups are excluded, targeted or underfunded.

This is why the current moment demands more than statements of concern. It demands political courage and structural change.

What needs to change — now

As governments, donors, and multilateral institutions navigate an era of uncertainty and reform, three urgent shifts are needed:

First, funding for feminist and women-led organisations must be protected and expanded, not sacrificed. Flexible, long-term, and core funding is essential for movements working under pressure — not short-term projects that ignore political realities on the ground.

Second, the enabling environment for civil society must be actively defended. This includes repealing restrictive laws, protecting women human rights defenders, and ensuring that digital spaces are governed with transparency, accountability, and gender equality at their core.

Third, women’s movements must be treated as political actors, not just beneficiaries. From UN reform debates to climate negotiations, from peacebuilding to digital governance, feminist civil society must have a seat at the table — with influence, not tokenism.

March with us: From solidarity to action

This is why Forus launched the March With Us campaign for gender justice, rooted in collective action, storytelling and feminist leadership.

March With Us is not about speaking for women; it is about creating space for women and gender-diverse activists to speak for themselves. Through articles, podcasts, multimedia storytelling, and speaking up in international advocacy spaces, the campaign amplifies voices that are too often excluded, despite being closest to the realities of injustice and resistance.

At a time when fear and fragmentation threaten to define our politics, feminist civil society continues to offer something radical: solidarity, care, accountability and hope.

This March, we choose to stand with them.

Because defending gender justice is not optional.

And because protecting women’s rights is nothing less than protecting democracy itself.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the critical role of feminist movements and women-led civil society organizations in defending democracy and human rights globally. It emphasizes the broad societal impact of gender justice on democratic resilience and calls for urgent structural changes to support these movements. The story is focused, detailed, and underscores a significant positive social cause with wide-reaching implications.
articles.personality_title: Women-led movements play a vital role in protecting democracy worldwide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around the world, groups led by women and feminist organizations have been working hard to protect human rights and support democracy. However, they now face many challenges like less funding, stricter laws, and online harassment.

**What happened** – This March, during International Women’s Day, these women-led groups are continuing to lead important movements despite growing difficulties. They often step in when governments fail, helping communities by defending rights, documenting abuses, and promoting fair policies. Yet, many of these organizations are being pushed to the sidelines and labeled as less important.

**Impact** – This situation is serious because when women organize and have a voice, societies become stronger and fairer. Without their involvement, democracy weakens and inequality grows. The article shows that supporting gender justice is not just about fairness but about keeping democracy healthy.

**What's next step** – Experts say three key changes are needed: more and better funding for women-led groups, protecting their rights and safety, and including them as equal partners in important decisions like climate talks and peacebuilding.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Supporting women-led organizations is essential for protecting democracy and building fairer societies around the world.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les mouvements dirigés par des femmes jouent un rôle clé pour protéger la démocratie dans le monde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans le monde entier, des groupes dirigés par des femmes et des organisations féministes travaillent dur pour défendre les droits humains et soutenir la démocratie. Cependant, ils font face à de nombreux défis comme la réduction des financements, des lois plus strictes et le harcèlement en ligne.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Ce mois de mars, à l’occasion de la Journée internationale des droits des femmes, ces groupes dirigés par des femmes continuent de mener des mouvements importants malgré des difficultés croissantes. Ils interviennent souvent lorsque les gouvernements échouent, aidant les communautés en défendant les droits, en documentant les abus et en promouvant des politiques justes. Pourtant, beaucoup de ces organisations sont mises à l’écart et considérées comme moins importantes.

**Impact** – Cette situation est grave car lorsque les femmes s’organisent et ont une voix, les sociétés deviennent plus fortes et plus justes. Sans leur participation, la démocratie s’affaiblit et les inégalités augmentent. L’article montre que soutenir la justice de genre n’est pas seulement une question d’équité, mais de santé démocratique.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les experts soulignent trois changements essentiels : plus de financements adaptés pour les groupes dirigés par des femmes, la protection de leurs droits et de leur sécurité, et leur inclusion en tant que partenaires égaux dans les décisions importantes comme les discussions sur le climat et la construction de la paix.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Soutenir les organisations dirigées par des femmes est essentiel pour protéger la démocratie et construire des sociétés plus justes dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Los movimientos liderados por mujeres juegan un papel vital para proteger la democracia en todo el mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En todo el mundo, grupos liderados por mujeres y organizaciones feministas trabajan arduamente para defender los derechos humanos y apoyar la democracia. Sin embargo, enfrentan muchos desafíos como menos financiamiento, leyes más estrictas y acoso en línea.

**Qué pasó** – Este marzo, durante el Día Internacional de la Mujer, estos grupos liderados por mujeres continúan liderando movimientos importantes a pesar de las crecientes dificultades. A menudo intervienen cuando los gobiernos fallan, ayudando a las comunidades defendiendo derechos, documentando abusos y promoviendo políticas justas. Sin embargo, muchas de estas organizaciones están siendo marginadas y consideradas menos importantes.

**Impacto** – Esta situación es grave porque cuando las mujeres se organizan y tienen voz, las sociedades se vuelven más fuertes y justas. Sin su participación, la democracia se debilita y la desigualdad crece. El artículo muestra que apoyar la justicia de género no es solo una cuestión de equidad, sino de salud democrática.

**Próximos pasos** – Los expertos dicen que se necesitan tres cambios clave: más y mejor financiamiento para los grupos liderados por mujeres, proteger sus derechos y seguridad, e incluirlas como socias iguales en decisiones importantes como las negociaciones climáticas y la construcción de la paz.

**Resumen en una frase** – Apoyar a las organizaciones lideradas por mujeres es esencial para proteger la democracia y construir sociedades más justas en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a diverse group of interlocking, stylized female and gender-diverse silhouettes formed from flowing ribbons of warm earth tones, standing united around a glowing, sturdy pillar symbolizing democracy, with subtle abstract motifs of digital networks and protective shields woven into the background, conveying solidarity, resilience, and the vital role of feminist movements in sustaining freedom and justice.
